title: Psychosis and Schizophrenia Care
subtitle: The nurse cares for a patient with psychosis — telling positive from negative symptoms, not arguing with delusions, presenting reality calmly, and checking command hallucinations for safety.

patient.name: Psychosis / Schizophrenia
patient.admitDate: The nurse supports a patient experiencing psychosis.

nursesNote: The nurse cares for a patient with psychosis, a loss of contact with reality seen in schizophrenia. Symptoms are grouped as positive (added experiences such as hallucinations, delusions, and disorganized speech or behavior) and negative (a loss of normal function such as flat affect, lack of motivation or pleasure, poverty of speech, and social withdrawal); positive symptoms generally respond better to medication, while negative symptoms are harder to treat. Delusions are fixed false beliefs: the nurse neither argues with nor agrees with them, but acknowledges the underlying feeling and calmly presents reality. Hallucinations are false sensory experiences, most often auditory; the nurse asks about their content and specifically assesses for command hallucinations telling the patient to harm self or others, because these are a safety priority. The nurse uses a calm, simple, consistent approach to build trust, avoids reinforcing the hallucination by pretending to see or hear it, and reduces stimulation. Antipsychotic medications reduce symptoms, must be taken consistently, and should not be stopped abruptly; the nurse watches for extrapyramidal side effects (tremor, rigidity, restlessness, acute dystonia) and for neuroleptic malignant syndrome (high fever, severe rigidity, altered mental status, unstable vital signs), which is a medical emergency. The nurse promotes safety, medication adherence, and connection to ongoing support.
providerOrders: Do not argue with or reinforce delusions; present reality calmly
Assess hallucination content, especially command hallucinations, for safety
Use a calm, simple, consistent approach and reduce stimulation
Give antipsychotics and monitor for EPS and neuroleptic malignant syndrome

opts.text: Hallucinations and delusions are positive symptoms
Flat affect and withdrawal are negative symptoms
Delusions are fixed false beliefs
The nurse should argue the patient out of delusions
Command hallucinations are a safety concern
A calm, consistent approach builds trust
Acknowledge the feeling, then present reality
Pretending to see the hallucination helps
Positive symptoms respond better to medication
Antipsychotics should not be stopped abruptly
High fever and rigidity may signal NMS
Reducing stimulation can help an agitated patient

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order responding to a patient reporting voices

opts.text: Ask what the voices are saying
Assess for command hallucinations and safety
Stay calm and acknowledge the patient's fear
Calmly present reality without arguing
Notify the team and document
Hearing voices that others do not hear
Showing little facial expression (flat affect)
Believing the TV sends secret messages
Lack of motivation and social withdrawal
Disorganized, jumbled speech
Speaking very little (poverty of speech)

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A patient says voices are telling him to hurt someone
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Stay calm and ensure immediate safety
Ask directly about the command and intent
Do not leave the patient alone; get help
Notify the provider and increase observation
Document the content and actions taken
Saying 'I don't hear the voices, but you seem scared'
Agreeing that the delusion is true
Using short, clear, calm statements
Arguing and demanding the patient stop believing it
Assessing command hallucinations for safety
Pretending to see what the patient sees
Encouraging consistent antipsychotic use
Ignoring new fever and rigidity on antipsychotics

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Psychosocial Integrity, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
